title: Minimum Conference Rooms (Stark Industry)

content.problemStatement:
JARVIS needs help managing meeting rooms at Stark Tower. Given N meetings with their start and end times, determine the minimum number of conference rooms required so that all meetings can be conducted without overlap. If a meeting ends at time X, another meeting can start at time X using the same room.


Input Format

First line contains TTT, number of test cases
For each test case:

First line contains NNN, number of meetings
Second line contains NNN space-separated integers denoting start times
Third line contains NNN space-separated integers denoting end times




Output Format

For each test case, print the minimum number of conference rooms needed.
constraints: All times are given in 24‑hour integer format
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N, the number of meetings. Second line contains N space‑separated integers denoting start times. Third line contains N space‑separated integers denoting end times. A meeting that ends at time X frees the room for another meeting that starts at time X.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the minimum number of conference rooms required.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Meetings are [1300‑1600], [1500‑1700], [1600‑1800]. At 1300 one room is used, at 1500 a second room is needed, at 1600 the first meeting ends and the third can reuse that room. Maximum concurrent meetings = 2.
Only one meeting, so only one room is required.
Both meetings occupy the same time interval, thus two rooms are needed simultaneously.

solution.approach: Sort all start times and all end times separately. Scan them with two pointers. Whenever the next meeting starts before the earliest ending meeting finishes, we need an extra room (rooms++). Otherwise, a meeting has finished and we can reuse a room (rooms--). The maximum value of rooms during the scan is the answer. This works because sorting puts events in chronological order and the two‑pointer scan mimics a sweep line over the timeline.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting, O(N) for the scan.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space for the two sorted arrays.
